note_data.title: Work, energy and power
keyPoints: One radian is defined as the angle in the sector of a circle when the arc length equals the radius of the circle
Angular speed (ω) can be calculated using ω = v/r = 2π/T = 2πf, where v is linear speed, r is radius, T is time period, and f is frequency
An object moving in a circular path at constant speed has constantly changing velocity due to changing direction, therefore it must be accelerating
Centripetal acceleration always acts perpendicular to motion towards the centre of the circle and can be calculated using a = v²/r = ω²r
Centripetal force causes circular motion and is given by F = mv²/r = mω²r
Gravitational fields are areas where objects with mass experience non-contact forces, which can be uniform or radial
Newton's law of gravitation states F = Gm₁m₂/r², where force is directly proportional to the product of masses and inversely proportional to the square of distance between them
Gravitational field strength in a radial field follows g = GM/r², showing an inverse square relationship with distance

definitions.definition: The angle in the sector of a circle when the arc length of that sector is equal to the radius of the circle
The angle turned through by an object in any given direction in radians
The angle an object moves through per unit time
The acceleration that causes circular motion with constant angular speed, always acting perpendicular to motion towards the centre
The force that always acts perpendicular to the motion of an object towards the centre of the circle
The force per unit mass exerted by a gravitational field on an object

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Show all working, include units in final answer
Use given data to find the answer with clear method shown

modelAnswer.question: A 2.0 kg mass is lifted vertically through a height of 3.0 m in 5.0 s. Calculate (a) the work done against gravity (b) the minimum power required.
modelAnswer.answer: (a) Work done = mgh = 2.0 × 9.8 × 3.0 = 59 J (b) Power = W/t = 59/5.0 = 12 W

commonMistakes: Forgetting that an object moving at constant speed in a circle is still accelerating due to changing direction
Confusing linear speed with angular speed - remember v = ωr relates them
Not recognizing that centripetal force always acts towards the center, perpendicular to motion
Mixing up the inverse square relationship in gravitational field strength - if distance doubles, field strength decreases by factor of 4
Forgetting that the mass of a uniform sphere acts as a point mass at its centre for gravitational calculations
examinerTips: Remember to convert between degrees and radians using the factor π/180
Always check that centripetal acceleration and force are directed towards the centre of the circular path
For gravitational problems, clearly identify whether you're dealing with uniform or radial fields as the equations differ
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Force-displacement graph showing work done as area under the curve, with force on y-axis and displacement on x-axis
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to calculate the power of a motor lifting a load and explain why efficiency is less than 100%
N/D 2022 P1 Q12 (1 mark) — multiple choice question on the unit of power, many confused watts with joules
M/J 2022 P2 Q3 (5 marks) — calculation of gravitational potential energy and kinetic energy for a falling object, required use of conservation of energy
N/D 2023 P2 Q5 (4 marks) — work done against friction when pulling a box up an incline, students had to identify all energy transfers

workedExample.question: A crane lifts a load of mass 500 kg through a vertical height of 12 m in 20 s. Calculate (a) the work done against gravity (b) the power of the crane. Take g = 10 m/s². (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify the formula for work done against gravity: W = mgh
Step 2: Substitute values: W = 500 kg × 10 m/s² × 12 m = 60,000 J
Step 3: Identify the formula for power: P = W/t
Step 4: Substitute values: P = 60,000 J ÷ 20 s = 3000 W
workedExample.answer: (a) Work done = mgh = 500 × 10 × 12 = 60,000 J (b) Power = Work done/time = 60,000/20 = 3000 W
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct formula, 1 mark for substitution with units, 1 mark for power formula, 1 mark for final answer with correct unit

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused energy and power units' — always use joules (J) for energy/work and watts (W) for power, never mix them up
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently forgot to include the time factor in power calculations' — remember power = work done/time taken, time must always be included
Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates often ignored friction and air resistance in energy problems' — always consider all forces acting and energy losses when applying conservation of energy
note_data.keyDiagram: Energy transfer diagram showing a block on an incline with arrows indicating: gravitational potential energy (mgh) at top, kinetic energy (½mv²) during motion, work done against friction (f × d) as heat, and work done by applied force (F × d). Include labels for height h, distance d, and all energy values with correct units.
note_data.quickRecap: Circular motion involves objects moving in circular paths with constantly changing velocity due to changing direction, requiring centripetal acceleration and force directed towards the centre. Angular quantities like angular speed relate to linear quantities through the radius of the path. Gravitational fields cause non-contact forces on masses, with field strength following an inverse square relationship with distance in radial fields.
